The **Medellín Metro** ([Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language"): *Metro de Medellín*) is a [rapid transit](/wiki/Rapid_transit "Rapid transit") system that crosses the [Metropolitan Area of Medellín](/wiki/Metropolitan_Area_of_Medell%C3%ADn "Metropolitan Area of Medellín") from North to South and from Centre to West. It first opened for service on 30 November 1995.[[4]](#cite_note-history-4) As one of the first implementations of modern mass transportation in [Colombia](/wiki/Colombia "Colombia") and the only metro system in the country, the Medellín Metro is a product of the urban planning of the [Antioquia department](/wiki/Antioquia_department "Antioquia department") of Colombia. It is part of the Aburrá Valley Integrated Transport System (*Sistema Integrado de Transporte del Valle de Aburrá*, [SITVA](/wiki/SITVA "SITVA")).

The city of [Medellín](/wiki/Medell%C3%ADn "Medellín") and its urban complex (ten cities in the [Aburrá Valley](/wiki/Aburr%C3%A1_Valley "Aburrá Valley")) had a period of relatively recent industrial development that started in the 1930s. The streetcar (*tranvía*) at the beginning of the 20th century can be considered a predecessor of the current Medellín Metro. The company known in Spanish as *Empresa de Transporte Masivo del Valle de Aburrá* - *Metro de Medellín Ltda* was created on 31 May 1979.[[4]](#cite_note-history-4)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Medell%C3%ADn_Metro&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e2/2018_Medell%C3%ADn_Tren_del_metro_con_direcci%C3%B3n_San_Antonio.jpg/330px-2018_Medell%C3%ADn_Tren_del_metro_con_direcci%C3%B3n_San_Antonio.jpg)](/wiki/File:2018_Medell%C3%ADn_Tren_del_metro_con_direcci%C3%B3n_San_Antonio.jpg)

Train of Line B.

The railway history of Colombia and Antioquia has not been indifferent to the industrialization process that started at the end of the 19th century and that only has been restrained by the social and political conflicts of this South American nation.

The [Antioquia](/wiki/Antioquia_Department "Antioquia Department") Department, and the [Paisa Region](/wiki/Paisa_Region "Paisa Region") in general, owe their progress to the construction of railways that put them in direct contact with the rest of the country (especially with [Bogotá](/wiki/Bogot%C3%A1 "Bogotá"), [Cali](/wiki/Cali "Cali") and the Colombian Caribbean Littoral).

Although the famous [Antioquia Railway](/wiki/Antioquia_Railway "Antioquia Railway") came to a decline and is now only remembered by the so-called *towns of the train*, an urban railway system received the attention of the region. In the same way Antioquia's Railways had a century ago, the Medellín Metro became an important social, cultural and development axis in one of the most important cities of Colombia and South America.

The city's speedy urban growth, especially since the 1960s, has filled the entire Aburrá Valley and made towns touch its borders: [Bello](/wiki/Bello_(Colombia) "Bello (Colombia)"), [Copacabana](/wiki/Copacabana,_Antioquia "Copacabana, Antioquia"), [Girardota](/wiki/Girardota "Girardota"), [Barbosa](/wiki/Barbosa,_Antioquia "Barbosa, Antioquia"), [Envigado](/wiki/Envigado "Envigado"), [Itagüí](/wiki/Itag%C3%BC%C3%AD "Itagüí"), San Antonio de Prado, [La Estrella](/wiki/La_Estrella_(Colombia) "La Estrella (Colombia)"), [Sabaneta](/wiki/Sabaneta,_Antioquia "Sabaneta, Antioquia") and [Caldas](/wiki/Caldas_(Antioquia) "Caldas (Antioquia)"), among others. With the growth of the city placing Medellín among the most economically important cities in the nation, local leaders were compelled to view the city as a complex, urban system comparable to other industrialized cities in the world, rather than as a provincial town.

In the same sense, [Medellín](/wiki/Medell%C3%ADn "Medellín") and its Metropolitan Area had to face the appearance of cartels during the 1970s, which produced serious problems of urban violence exacerbated by speedy urban growth and slow answers to the needs of the surrounding communities. The city grew due to big waves of migrants coming from the Colombian countryside looking for refuge from internal political conflict. This background explains why the young city would face urban violence with the same intensity as large metropolitan areas such as [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), [Mexico City](/wiki/Mexico_City "Mexico City") or [Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro "Rio de Janeiro") and why the city had to create urban projects in answer to its conflicts and growth. The Medellín Metro was created not only as a massive urban transport solution for the working class residents of the city, but also as an important cultural symbol that would help develop marginalized sectors.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The Metro would change the concept of public space in a city built for business and factories that had the systematic issue of lacking space for things like tourism.

As a company, the Medellín Metro was created for the administration and operation of the Metro system. It was founded with the association of the [Medellín](/wiki/Medell%C3%ADn "Medellín") Municipality and the [Antioquia](/wiki/Antioquia_Department "Antioquia Department") Government. In 1979, research on economic and technical possibilities began, performed by the company *[Mott, Hay and Anderson](/wiki/Mott,_Hay_and_Anderson "Mott, Hay and Anderson") Ltd*.

In 1980 the project was presented to the National Government, and in 1982 it was approved by the National Council of Economic and Social Policies. It also gave the company an external contract of 100% of the required resources for the work. In 1984 the company subcontracted German and Spanish firms.

On 30 November 1995,[[2]](#cite_note-Datos_del_sistema-2)[[4]](#cite_note-history-4) 11:00 (local time), the first journey between [Niquía](/wiki/Niqu%C3%ADa_station "Niquía station") and [Poblado](/wiki/Poblado_station "Poblado station") Stations began. The first phase of the metro network was completed in 1996.

The citizens soon welcomed the new service, and the social and cultural impact was significant. The Medellín Metro soon became a symbol of the city (it was the first, and still the only, rail-based Metro system in Colombia) which encouraged tourism and new business growth in areas of the city. There were visitors first from other regions and cities of Colombia and afterwards from abroad. Importantly, the metro bridged previously disparate poor urban and wealthy urban areas. The Metro passes through districts with widely varied socio-economic compositions. For example, it passes through both "Lovaina" and "Poblado".

Commuters also saw a vast improvement in transit times. For example, a two-hour bus trip from [Bello](/wiki/Bello,_Antioquia "Bello, Antioquia") to [Envigado](/wiki/Envigado "Envigado") requires only 30 minutes on the Metro.

## Network

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Medell%C3%ADn_Metro&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Network")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f7/Palacio_de_la_Cultura-Medellin.JPG/250px-Palacio_de_la_Cultura-Medellin.JPG)](/wiki/File:Palacio_de_la_Cultura-Medellin.JPG)

[Berrío Park station](/wiki/Berr%C3%ADo_Park_station "Berrío Park station")

The Medellín Metro currently comprises two lines: *Line A*, which is 25.8 kilometres (16.0 mi) long and serves 21 stations, and *Line B*, which is 5.5 kilometres (3.4 mi) long and serves 6 stations (plus San Antonio station, the transfer station with Line A). There is also a tram line: *Line T-A* ([Ayacucho Tram](/wiki/Ayacucho_Tram "Ayacucho Tram")).[[2]](#cite_note-Datos_del_sistema-2)[[5]](#cite_note-:0-5)

Additionally, the aerial [cable car](/wiki/Aerial_lift "Aerial lift") system, [Metrocable](/wiki/Metrocable_(Medell%C3%ADn) "Metrocable (Medellín)"), which supplements the Metro system, comprises five lines: *Line J* with 3 stations (plus one transfer station with Metro Line B),[[1]](#cite_note-mapa-1)[[2]](#cite_note-Datos_del_sistema-2) *Line K* with 3 stations (plus one transfer station with Line L),[[2]](#cite_note-Datos_del_sistema-2)[[5]](#cite_note-:0-5) *Line L* with one station (plus one transfer station with Line K), *Line H* with two stations (plus one transfer station with Line T-A), *Line M* with two stations (plus one transfer station with Line T-A) and *Line P* with three stations (plus one transfer station with Line A).[[5]](#cite_note-:0-5)

### Expansions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Medell%C3%ADn_Metro&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Expansions")]

#### Metrocable

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Medell%C3%ADn_Metro&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Metrocable")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2b/2018_L%C3%ADnea_H_del_Metrocable_entre_las_estaciones_Las_Torres_y_Villa_Sierra_-_MEdell%C3%ADn.jpg/250px-2018_L%C3%ADnea_H_del_Metrocable_entre_las_estaciones_Las_Torres_y_Villa_Sierra_-_MEdell%C3%ADn.jpg)](/wiki/File:2018_L%C3%ADnea_H_del_Metrocable_entre_las_estaciones_Las_Torres_y_Villa_Sierra_-_MEdell%C3%ADn.jpg)

Line H of the [Metrocable](/wiki/Metrocable_(Medellin) "Metrocable (Medellin)")

On 7 August 2004,[[4]](#cite_note-history-4) the city inaugurated a new line known as "Metro Cable" (Line K). The line starts in the Acevedo Station and goes to the up hill district of Santo Domingo Savio.[[6]](#cite_note-Metrocable-6) This important addition integrated new additions to the city that since the 1960s that previously were not considered part of the "real city".

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/5/58/Metrocablemed1.png/250px-Metrocablemed1.png)](/wiki/File:Metrocablemed1.png)

Line K ([Metrocable](/wiki/Metrocable_(Medellin) "Metrocable (Medellin)")) of the Metro de Medellín.

On 3 March 2008,[[4]](#cite_note-history-4) a second "Metro Cable" line (Line J) was inaugurated. The line starts in the San Javier Station and goes through Juan XXIII and Vallejuelos to the La Aurora district.[[6]](#cite_note-Metrocable-6) This new line benefits approximately 150,000 new users.

A new Metrocable line (line L) was inaugurated in 2009[[4]](#cite_note-history-4) with a transfer station at Santo Domingo Savio Station. This line continues further uphill to El Tambo[[6]](#cite_note-Metrocable-6) in Arví park near [Guarne](/wiki/Guarne "Guarne"). The reason for constructing this line is because the city wants to promote tourism in the rural area near Lake Guarne. It takes 14 minutes to ascend to El Tambo and there are no [intermediate stations](/wiki/Intermediate_stations "Intermediate stations").

#### Line A extension

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Medell%C3%ADn_Metro&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Line A extension")]

Line A was expanded from [Itagüí](/wiki/Itag%C3%BC%C3%AD "Itagüí") to [La Estrella](/wiki/La_Estrella,_Colombia "La Estrella, Colombia"), in the south of the metropolitan area. A new intermediate station, [Sabaneta](/wiki/Sabaneta_station "Sabaneta station"), built near 67th South Street, was opened on 5 August 2012[[7]](#cite_note-7) and the final station, [La Estrella](/wiki/La_Estrella_station "La Estrella station"), was built near 77th South Street and opened on 17 September 2012.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

#### Train line

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Medell%C3%ADn_Metro&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Train line")]

In February 2020 it was announced that Medellín will reactivate the train line between Bello and Caldas.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

## Rolling stock

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Medell%C3%ADn_Metro&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Rolling stock")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c1/Metro_de_Medell%C3%ADn-Arturito.JPG/120px-Metro_de_Medell%C3%ADn-Arturito.JPG)](/wiki/File:Metro_de_Medell%C3%ADn-Arturito.JPG)

*Arturito* at [Berrío Park station](/wiki/Berr%C3%ADo_Park_station "Berrío Park station").

Initially, there were 42 three-car train sets built by the manufacturer [MAN](/wiki/MAN_SE "MAN SE"), however, in 2009, the Medellín Metro signed a contract with Spanish manufacturer [CAF](/wiki/Construcciones_y_Auxiliar_de_Ferrocarriles "Construcciones y Auxiliar de Ferrocarriles") for 12 three-car train sets that were delivered between that year and 2011. In 2017, the Medellín Metro purchased another set of 26 CAF trains to complement their fleet, with 38 CAF trains in total, and 80 trains with both the MAN and CAF fleet.
